title: Architect Rounds
description: Architecture tradeoffs, platform strategy, reliability, observability, governance, vendor selection, and cost.

questions.title: At architecture level, how do you decide between RAG and fine-tuning?
questions.scenario: A principal architect review board runs enterprise GenAI architecture spanning assistants, RAG, model routing, and governance. A recent review found that architecture choices optimize local delivery but create platform-level risk; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for At architecture level, how do you decide between RAG and fine-tuning measured by SLO compliance, cost per task, incident rate, audit readiness, and adoption.
questions.expectedAnswer: At architecture level, how do you decide between RAG and fine-tuning requires comparing at least two viable choices instead of assuming one technique is universally better. In enterprise GenAI architecture spanning assistants, RAG, model routing, and governance, the practical approach is retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from architecture decisions, provider metrics, cost dashboards, traces, and risk assessments, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is retrieving plausible but unsupported context; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use SLO compliance, cost per task, incident rate, audit readiness, and adoption, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to retrieving plausible but unsupported context. At architecture level, how do you decide between RAG and fine-tuning addresses that risk through retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using SLO compliance, cost per task, incident rate, audit readiness, and adoption.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from architecture decisions, provider metrics, cost dashboards, traces, and risk assessments. Implement the change with Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in enterprise GenAI architecture spanning assistants, RAG, model routing, and governance, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor SLO compliance, cost per task, incident rate, audit readiness, and adoption so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

questions.title: How would you explain platform roadmap planning in a Architect Rounds interview?
questions.scenario: A principal architect review board runs enterprise GenAI architecture spanning assistants, RAG, model routing, and governance. questions.expectedAnswer: platform roadmap planning belongs in the architecture discussion because it affects ownership boundaries, controls, observability, and rollout. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from architecture decisions, provider metrics, cost dashboards, traces, and risk assessments, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using domain datasets, baselines, monitoring, evaluation dashboards, and human review, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures.
